title: Conditional Probability
concept: Conditional probability calculates the likelihood of an event occurring given that another event has already happened.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What shape does a normal distribution make when plotted?
exercises.explain: A normal distribution is characterized by its signature symmetric bell-shaped curve.

cards.title: Will it rain today?
cards.body: What is the chance it rains today? Maybe 10%. But what if you look out the window and see dark, heavy clouds? Suddenly, the chance of rain jumps to 90%. The new information changed the odds.

cards.title: Probability given...
cards.body: Conditional probability is the likelihood of event A happening, given that event B has already occurred. We write this as P(A | B), read as 'probability of A given B'.

cards.title: Filtering a deck of cards
cards.body: Draw a random card from a deck; the chance it's a King is 4/52. But if someone tells you the card is a face card (Jack, Queen, King), you throw out all number cards. The chance it's a King rises to 4/12.

cards.title: How spam filters work
cards.body: An email containing the word 'Winner' has a much higher chance of being spam than a generic email. ML spam filters calculate P(Spam | 'Winner'): the probability an email is spam, given that it contains the word 'Winner'.

cards.title: Order matters!
cards.body: P(A | B) is NOT the same as P(B | A). For example, the probability you are a doctor given that you wear a white coat is high. But the probability you wear a white coat given that you are a doctor is different.

cards.title: Shrinking the possibilities
cards.body: How condition B changes the probability of event A:

left.text:
Compare A to the whole world
All possibilities are open

right.text:
Compare A only to world B
Throw away anything outside B

exercises.prompt: How do we write 'the probability that a user clicks an ad, given that they are on a mobile device' using conditional probability notation?
exercises.explain: The vertical bar | stands for 'given that'. P(Click | Mobile) means the probability of Clicking, given Mobile.

exercises.prompt: True or false: P(Rain | Cloudy) is always equal to P(Cloudy | Rain).
exercises.explain: False! If it rains, it is almost certainly cloudy (high P(Cloudy | Rain)). But if it is cloudy, it doesn't always rain (lower P(Rain | Cloudy)). Order matters!

exercises.sentence: Conditional probability is about shrinking the set of possible outcomes to only focus on when event ___ happens.
exercises.explain: In P(A | B), event B is the condition. We filter out any outcomes where B did not happen, shrinking our denominator.

exercises.prompt: A streaming service finds that 20% of all users watch sci-fi. However, among users who watch space documentaries (B), 80% watch sci-fi (A). What is P(sci-fi | space documentary)?
exercises.explain: The condition is watching space documentaries. Given that condition, the probability of watching sci-fi is 80%.

exercises.prompt: You are designing an autonomous vehicle sensor. The probability of the brake sensor failing in general is 1%. However, if the car is driving in heavy rain, the probability of failure increases to 5%. How should the AI system model this risk?

options.text: Model the risk as P(Failure | Rain) = 5% to dynamically adjust safety margins during rain.
Always assume a flat 1% failure rate regardless of weather.
Use P(Rain | Failure) to adjust safety margins.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The AI must use the conditional probability of failure given rain to adjust its safety systems when it detects rain. Outstanding work reasoning through conditions!
